primary_diagnosis.name: Rheumatic heart disease - severe mitral stenosis with acute decompensation (pulmonary venous congestion / impending pulmonary oedema and haemoptysis) and new rapid atrial fibrillation

comorbidities.name: Atrial fibrillation (new/poorly controlled, rapid ventricular response)

pathophysiology_summary: Rheumatic mitral stenosis is a late consequence of acute rheumatic fever: an autoimmune reaction to group A streptococcal pharyngitis in childhood scars the mitral valve, causing leaflet thickening, commissural fusion and, over decades, progressive calcific narrowing of the mitral orifice. The obstructed valve impedes left atrial emptying, so left atrial pressure rises and is transmitted backwards into the pulmonary veins and capillaries, producing pulmonary venous hypertension. This explains the slowly progressive exertional dyspnoea over decades and, when the atrium acutely decompensates, orthopnoea, rest dyspnoea and interstitial/alveolar pulmonary oedema. Haemoptysis arises from rupture of engorged bronchial/pulmonary venous anastomoses under the raised pressure. Left atrial dilatation and stretch predispose to atrial fibrillation; the loss of atrial systole and a rapid ventricular rate abruptly shorten diastolic filling time across the stenotic valve, sharply raising left atrial pressure and precipitating acute decompensation - the trigger here. The malar flush reflects low cardiac output with peripheral cyanosis. On examination the classic signs are a loud S1, an opening snap and a low-pitched mid-diastolic rumbling murmur at the apex, none obtainable over chat. Atrial fibrillation with stasis in the dilated left atrium also carries a high risk of systemic (including cerebral) embolism. Definitive assessment needs echocardiography (valve area, gradient, calcification) and an ECG; acute management is oxygen, IV diuresis, rate control and urgent hospital admission, with later valvuloplasty or valve replacement. None of this can be delivered by telehealth, so the correct action is an emergency ambulance.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated rheumatic mitral stenosis with pulmonary congestion
differential.evidence_basis: Decades of progressive exertional dyspnoea now acutely worse with orthopnoea and haemoptysis, on a background suggestive of rheumatic valve disease, is a classic decompensated mitral stenosis pattern.
Acute breathlessness with haemoptysis raises PE, but the decades-long progressive history and rheumatic background point to valvular obstruction rather than acute embolism.
Cough and breathlessness could suggest infection, but there is no fever or purulent sputum and the picture is cardiogenic congestion.
Another rheumatic valve lesion causing breathlessness, but the auscultatory pattern (loud S1, opening snap, mid-diastolic rumble) is that of stenosis, not regurgitation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat acute rest breathlessness with orthopnoea and haemoptysis as a cardiopulmonary emergency requiring an ambulance, while keeping PE on the differential.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated severe rheumatic mitral stenosis with rapid atrial fibrillation and pulmonary congestion
differential.evidence_basis: Rest dyspnoea/orthopnoea (DI-001, DI-003) + haemoptysis (DI-002) + new irregular rapid heartbeat (DI-004) + childhood rheumatic fever (DI-005) together strongly indicate decompensated mitral stenosis precipitated by new atrial fibrillation.
Remains to be excluded on work-up given haemoptysis, but the structural/valvular history and orthopnoea favour cardiogenic congestion.
No fever or purulent sputum; the picture is cardiogenic pulmonary congestion, not infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should be confident of decompensated mitral stenosis with new AF needing an emergency ambulance for oxygen, IV diuresis, rate control and hospital cardiology assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Decompensated severe rheumatic mitral stenosis with rapid atrial fibrillation and impending pulmonary oedema - presumptive, pending ECG and echocardiography in hospital
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis. Confirmation requires ECG (atrial fibrillation) and echocardiography (mitral valve area, gradient, calcification); treatment is oxygen, IV diuresis, rate control and urgent admission, with later valvuloplasty or valve replacement.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a life-threatening acute decompensation of severe mitral stenosis requiring an immediate 000 ambulance - explicitly NOT waiting until morning or self-managing at home.

red_flags.description: Acute severe breathlessness at rest, unable to complete sentences
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rest dyspnoea with inability to speak in full sentences indicates severe acute cardiopulmonary compromise requiring emergency response.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to frank pulmonary oedema and respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Haemoptysis (pink, blood-tinged sputum) in the setting of acute breathlessness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Haemoptysis with rest dyspnoea signals raised pulmonary venous pressure (or must exclude PE) and is an emergency feature mandating urgent hospital assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed pulmonary venous hypertension/oedema or an alternative life-threatening cause of haemoptysis.

red_flags.description: Orthopnoea - cannot lie flat, feels she is drowning, needs multiple pillows
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orthopnoea is a hallmark of pulmonary congestion and left-sided/valvular heart failure requiring urgent management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Underestimation of the severity of pulmonary congestion.

red_flags.description: New rapid, irregular heartbeat (new atrial fibrillation) driving decompensation
red_flags.clinical_significance: New rapid AF shortens diastolic filling across the stenotic valve, precipitating acute decompensation, and carries a high embolic/stroke risk requiring urgent rate control and assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing rapid ventricular rate worsens congestion and untreated AF risks thromboembolic stroke.

red_flags.description: History of childhood rheumatic fever (predisposing valvular injury)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Childhood rheumatic fever is the key predisposing cause of mitral stenosis and greatly raises the pre-test probability of a rheumatic valve lesion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to weight the rheumatic aetiology lowers suspicion for mitral stenosis and anchors on PE or infection.

red_flags.description: Loud S1, opening snap and low-pitched mid-diastolic apical rumbling murmur, bibasal crackles, malar flush, irregularly irregular pulse and bilateral ankle oedema on clinician examination; low SpO2 and tachypnoea on measured vitals
red_flags.clinical_significance: The classic auscultatory triad with congestion signs confirms decompensated mitral stenosis on examination - all clinician-elicited and not obtainable over chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these require clinician examination; the correct action is an emergency ambulance and hospital assessment.

red_flags.description: ECG confirming atrial fibrillation and echocardiography showing a stenosed, calcified/fused mitral valve - pending in hospital
red_flags.clinical_significance: ECG and echocardiography are the definitive investigations that confirm AF and quantify mitral stenosis; they can only be obtained in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires in-hospital investigation; the correct action is urgent admission.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on pulmonary embolism from the haemoptysis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Haemoptysis with breathlessness readily suggests PE, but the decades-long progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea and childhood rheumatic fever point to pulmonary venous congestion from mitral stenosis; both warrant emergency assessment, so the escalation is the same either way.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dismissing acute-on-chronic breathlessness as the patient's usual complaint
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Because the breathlessness is long-standing, an abrupt decompensation can be minimised as a chronic flare rather than recognised as an emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to elicit the rheumatic fever history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The childhood rheumatic fever is a pivotal predisposing clue gated behind a specific question; not asking it lowers suspicion for mitral stenosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-recognising new atrial fibrillation as the decompensation trigger and a stroke risk
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A new rapid irregular heartbeat is easily dismissed as palpitations, missing both the precipitant of decompensation and the high thromboembolic risk in mitral stenosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising private-car transport or waiting until morning
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A patient breathless at rest with haemoptysis can deteriorate suddenly and needs oxygen and monitoring; anything short of a 000 ambulance is an under-triage.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the loud S1, opening snap and mid-diastolic apical rumbling murmur
Chest auscultation for bibasal crackles of pulmonary congestion
Measured vital signs including respiratory rate, oxygen saturation and pulse rhythm
Echocardiography to demonstrate the stenosed, calcified/fused mitral valve and assess severity
Chest X-ray to assess pulmonary congestion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate emergency ambulance (000) to hospital for oxygen, IV diuresis, rate control of atrial fibrillation, monitoring and urgent cardiology/echocardiography assessment - do NOT advise waiting until morning, self-adjusting diuretics, or private-car transport.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient this is her usual breathlessness, or advising an extra water tablet and review in the morning, risks progression to frank pulmonary oedema, respiratory failure, or thromboembolic stroke from untreated rapid atrial fibrillation.
